name: King county, Montana
description:
Welcome to King County, a quiet, rural stretch of Montana where life moves at its own pace. The year is 1985, and while the rest of the country is swept up in neon lights, big hair, and corporate dreams, King County remains largely untouched—a relic of an older, grittier America. This roleplay delves into the lives of the people who call this place home, from ranchers and mechanics to drifters and outlaws. It’s a world of small-town gossip, deep-seated grudges, and a quiet tension that never quite fades.

King County is a land of contrasts: picturesque landscapes hiding bleak realities, close-knit communities masking old rivalries, and hardworking folks rubbing shoulders with the restless and the lost. Out here, trust is earned, secrets don’t stay buried, and everyone knows your name. Whether you're a local trying to keep your family business afloat, a lawman barely holding the peace, or a stranger looking for a fresh start, your story unfolds in a town that never really changes—until it does.

Lore of King County

King County is centered around Black Butte, the largest town in the county with a population of just over 4,000. It’s the kind of place where the diner waitress knows how you take your coffee, the sheriff drinks at the same bar as the troublemakers, and the biggest events of the year are the county fair and high school football games. But beneath its seemingly simple surface, Black Butte has its fair share of mysteries, tragedies, and tensions waiting to boil over.

The local economy is driven by ranching, mining, and logging, with a few factories providing stable—if grueling—employment. The town’s older residents still talk about the Silver Rush of the late 1800s, when King County was briefly flooded with prospectors chasing wealth. Now, most of the old mines are abandoned, left to cave in or serve as hideouts for people with nowhere else to go.

Crime in King County is low but persistent. Most of it is petty theft, bar fights, or moonshine operations out in the woods, but every few years, something bigger shakes the town—an unsolved murder, a disappearance, a wildfire that maybe wasn’t so accidental. The Sheriff’s Department, led by the weary but stubborn Sheriff Eli Dunbar, does its best to keep the peace, but with limited resources and miles of open country, justice is often slow and unreliable.

The railroad still runs through town, though fewer trains stop these days. Those who do hop off in Black Butte are usually drifters, looking for work or a place to disappear. The roads are long, the winters are harsh, and the nearest real city is Billings, two hours away. King County is the kind of place where people either stay for life or leave and never come back.

Life in King County

Living in King County means knowing how to survive. The winters are brutal, jobs are hard, and the land itself seems determined to wear people down. The people here are tough, stubborn, and proud, shaped by generations of hardship.

  • The Butte View Diner is the heart of Black Butte, where truckers, ranchers, and factory workers all come for a cheap meal and a strong cup of coffee.

  • McClellan’s General Store has been family-run for decades, stocked with everything from canned goods to hunting rifles.

  • The Rusty Spur Saloon is the place for a stiff drink, a bad decision, and maybe a fight if you stay long enough.

  • Old Timer’s Mine is officially abandoned, but rumors swirl about what—or who—might be down there.

  • The Fairgrounds host rodeos, flea markets, and the annual King County Fair, the biggest event of the year.

Every town has its secrets, and Black Butte is no exception. Some folks say the abandone
